sections.label: Section 2.860
sections.original: πῶς δ ’ ὄμματα προσβαλοῦσα
sections.translation: How wilt thou look upon thy babes , and still without a tear retain thy bloody purpose ? Thou canst not , when they fall at thy feet for mercy , steel thy heart and dip

sections.label: Section 2.865
sections.original: τλάμονι θυμῷ .
sections.translation: in their blood thy hand .

sections.label: Section 866
sections.original: ἥκω κελευσθείς · καὶ γὰρ οὖσα δυσμενὴς
sections.translation: I am come at thy bidding , for e ’ en though thy hate for me is bitter thou shalt not fail in this small boon , but I will hear what new request thou hast to make of me , lady .

sections.label: Section 869
sections.original: Ἰᾶσον , αἰτοῦμαί σε τῶν εἰρημένων
sections.translation: Jason , I crave thy pardon

sections.label: Section 870
sections.original: συγγνώμον ’ εἶναι · τὰς δ ’ ἐμὰς ὀργὰς φέρειν
sections.translation: for the words I spoke , and well thou mayest brook my burst of passion , for ere now we twain have shared much love . For I have reasoned with my soul and railed upon me thus , Ah ! poor heart 1 why am I thus distraught , why so angered ’ gainst all good advice ,

sections.label: Section 875
sections.original: ἐχθρὰ δὲ γαίας κοιράνοις καθίσταμαι
sections.translation: why have I come to hate the rulers of the land , my husband too , who does the best for me he can , in wedding with a princess and rearing for my children noble brothers ? Shall I not cease to fret ? What possesses me , when heaven its best doth offer ?

sections.label: Section 880
sections.original: οὐκ εἰσὶ μέν μοι παῖδες , οἶδα δὲ χθόνα
sections.translation: Have I not my children to consider ? do I forget that we are fugitives , in need of friends ? When I had thought all this I saw how foolish I had been , how senselessly enraged . So now I do commend thee and think thee most wise in forming

sections.label: Section 885
sections.original: κῆδος τόδ ’ ἡμῖν προσλαβών , ἐγὼ δ ’ ἄφρων ,
sections.translation: this connexion for us ; but I was mad , I who should have shared in these designs , helped on thy plans , and lent my aid to bring about the match , only too pleased to wait upon thy bride . But what we are , we are , we women , evil I will not say ;

sections.label: Section 890
sections.original: γυναῖκες · οὔκουν χρῆν σ ’ ὁμοιοῦσθαι κακοῖς ,
sections.translation: wherefore thou shouldst not sink to our sorry level nor with our weapons meet our childishness .

sections.label: Section 892
sections.original: παριέμεσθα , καί φαμεν κακῶς φρονεῖν
sections.translation: I yield and do confess that I was wrong then , but now have I come to a better mind . Come hither , my children , come , leave the house ,

sections.label: Section 895
sections.original: ἐξέλθετ ’, ἀσπάσασθε καὶ προσείπατε
sections.translation: step forth , and with me greet and bid farewell to your father , be reconciled from all past bitterness unto your friends , as now your mother is ; for we have made a truce and anger is no more . Enter the Children . Take his right hand ; ah me ! my sad fate !

sections.label: Section 900
sections.original: ὡς ἐννοοῦμαι δή τι τῶν κεκρυμμένων .
sections.translation: when I reflect , as now , upon the hidden future . O my children , since there awaits you even thus a long , long life , stretch forth the hand to take a fond farewell . Ah me ! how new [*] to tears am I , how full of fear ! For now that I have at last released me from my quarrel with your father ,

sections.label: Section 905
sections.original: ὄψιν τέρειναν τήνδ ’ ἔπλησα δακρύων .
sections.translation: I let the tear-drops stream adown my tender cheek .

sections.label: Section 906
sections.original: κἀμοὶ κατ ’ ὄσσων χλωρὸν ὡρμήθη δάκρυ ·
sections.translation: From my eyes too bursts forth the copious tear ; O , may no greater ill than the present e ’ er befall !

sections.label: Section 908
sections.original: αἰνῶ , γύναι , τάδ ’, οὐδ ’ ἐκεῖνα μέμφομαι ·
sections.translation: Lady , I praise this conduct , not that I blame what is past ; for it is but natural to the female sex to vent their spleen against a husband

sections.label: Section 910
sections.original: γάμου παρεμπολῶντος ἀλλοίου πόσει .
sections.translation: when he trafficks in other marriages besides his own . [*] But thy heart is changed to wiser schemes and thou art determined on the better course , late though it be ; this is acting like a woman of sober sense . And for you , my sons , hath your father provided

sections.label: Section 915
sections.original: πολλὴν ἔθηκε σὺν θεοῖς σωτηρίαν ·
sections.translation: with all good heed a sure refuge , by God ’ s grace ; for ye , I trow , shall with your brothers share hereafter the foremost rank in this Corinthian realm . Only grow up , for all the rest your sire and whoso of the gods is kind to us is bringing to pass .

sections.label: Section 920
sections.original: ἴδοιμι δ ’ ὑμᾶς εὐτραφεῖς ἥβης τέλος
sections.translation: May I see you reach man ’ s full estate , high o ’ er the heads of those I hate ! But thou , lady , why with fresh tears dost thou thine eyelids wet , turning away thy wan cheek , with no welcome for these my happy tidings ?

sections.label: Section 925
sections.original: οὐδέν . τέκνων τῶνδ ’ ἐννοουμένη πέρι .
sections.translation: ’ Tis naught ; upon these children my thoughts were turned .

sections.label: Section 926
sections.original: θάρσει νυν · εὖ γὰρ τῶνδ ’ ἐγὼ θήσω πέρι .
sections.translation: Then take heart ; for I will see that it is well with them .

sections.label: Section 927
sections.original: δράσω τάδ ’· οὔτοι σοῖς ἀπιστήσω λόγοις ·
sections.translation: I will do so ; nor will I doubt thy word ; woman is a weak creature , ever given to tears .

sections.label: Section 929
sections.original: τί δῆτα λίαν τοῖσδ ’ ἐπιστένεις τέκνοις ;
sections.translation: Why prithee , unhappy one , dost moan o ’ er these children ?

sections.label: Section 930
sections.original: ἔτικτον αὐτούς · ζῆν δ ’ ὅτ ’ ἐξηύχου τέκνα ,
sections.translation: I gave them birth ; and when thou didst pray long life for them , pity entered into my soul to think that these things must be . But the reason of thy coming hither to speak with me is partly told , the rest will I now mention . Since it is the pleasure of the rulers of the land to banish me ,

sections.label: Section 935
sections.original: κἀμοὶ τάδ ’ ἐστὶ λῷστα , γιγνώσκω καλῶς ,
sections.translation: and well I know ’ twere best for me to stand not in the way of thee or of the rulers by dwelling here , enemy as I am thought unto their house , forth from this land in exile am I going , but these children ,— that they may know thy fostering hand ,

sections.label: Section 940
sections.original: αἰτοῦ Κρέοντα τήνδε μὴ φεύγειν χθόνα .
sections.translation: beg Creon to remit their banishment

sections.label: Section 941
sections.original: οὐκ οἶδ ’ ἂν εἰ πείσαιμι , πειρᾶσθαι δὲ χρή .
sections.translation: I doubt whether I can persuade him , yet must I attempt it .

sections.label: Section 942
sections.original: σὺ δ ’ ἀλλὰ σὴν κέλευσον αἰτεῖσθαι πατρὸς
sections.translation: At least do thou bid thy wife ask her sire this boon , to remit the exile of the children from this land .

sections.label: Section 944
sections.original: μάλιστα , καὶ πείσειν γε δοξάζω σφ ’ ἐγώ .
sections.translation: Yea , that will I ; and her methinks I shall persuade ,

sections.label: Section 945
sections.original: εἴπερ γυναικῶν ἐστι τῶν ἄλλων μία .
sections.translation: since she is a woman like the rest .

sections.label: Section 946
sections.original: συλλήψομαι δὲ τοῦδέ σοι κἀγὼ πόνου ·
sections.translation: I too will aid thee in this task , for by the children ’ s hand I will send to her gifts that far surpass in beauty , I well know , aught that now is seen ’ mongst men , a robe of finest tissue and a chaplet of chased gold .

sections.label: Section 950
sections.original: παῖδας φέροντας . ἀλλ ’ ὅσον τάχος χρεὼν
sections.translation: But one of my attendants must haste and bring the ornaments hither . Happy shall she be not once alone but ten thousandfold , for in thee she wins the noblest soul to share her love , and gets these gifts as well which on a day my father ’ s sire , the Sun-god ,

sections.label: Section 955
sections.original: πατρὸς πατὴρ δίδωσιν ἐκγόνοισιν οἷς .
sections.translation: bestowed on his descendants . My children , take in your hands these wedding gifts , and bear them as an offering to the royal maid , the happy bride ; for verily the gifts she shall receive are not to be scorned .

sections.label: Section 959
sections.original: τί δ ’, ὦ ματαία , τῶνδε σὰς κενοῖς χέρας ;
sections.translation: But why so rashly rob thyself of these gifts ?

sections.label: Section 960
sections.original: δοκεῖς σπανίζειν δῶμα βασίλειον πέπλων ,
sections.translation: Dost think a royal palace wants for robes or gold ? Keep them , nor give them to another . For well I know that if my lady hold me in esteem , she will set my price above all wealth .

sections.label: Section 964
sections.original: μή μοι σύ · πείθειν δῶρα καὶ θεοὺς λόγος ·
sections.translation: Say not so ; ’ tis said that gifts tempt even gods ;

sections.label: Section 965
sections.original: χρυσὸς δὲ κρείσσων μυρίων λόγων βροτοῖς .
sections.translation: and o ’ er men ’ s minds gold holds more potent sway than countless words . Fortune smiles upon thy bride , and heaven now doth swell her triumph ; youth is hers and princely power ; yet to save my children from exile I would barter life , not dross alone . Children , when ye are come to the rich palace ,

sections.label: Section 970
sections.original: πατρὸς νέαν γυναῖκα , δεσπότιν δ ’ ἐμήν ,
sections.translation: pray your father ’ s new bride , my mistress , with suppliant voice to save you from exile , offering her these ornaments the while ; for it is most needful that she receive the gifts in her own hand . Now go and linger not ; may ye succeed and to your mother bring back

sections.label: Section 975
sections.original: εὐάγγελοι γένοισθε πράξαντες καλῶς .
sections.translation: the glad tidings she fain would hear !

sections.label: Section 1.976
sections.original: νῦν ἐλπίδες οὐκέτι μοι παίδων ζόας ,
sections.translation: Gone , gone is every hope I had that the children yet might live ; forth to their doom they now proceed . The hapless bride will take , ay , take the golden crown that is to be her ruin ;

sections.label: Section 1.980
sections.original: ξανθᾷ δ ’ ἀμφὶ κόμᾳ θήσει τὸν Ἅιδα
sections.translation: with her own hand will she lift and place upon her golden locks the garniture of death .

sections.label: Section 1.983
sections.original: πείσει χάρις ἀμβρόσιός τ ’ αὐγὰ πέπλων
sections.translation: Its grace and sheen divine will tempt her to put on the robe and crown of gold ,

sections.label: Section 1.985
sections.original: νερτέροις δ ’ ἤδη πάρα νυμφοκομήσει .
sections.translation: and in that act will she deck herself to be a bride amid the dead . Such is the snare whereinto she will fall , such is the deadly doom that waits the hapless maid , nor shall she from the curse escape .

sections.label: Section 2.990
sections.original: σὺ δ ’, ὦ τάλαν , ὦ κακόνυμφε κηδεμὼν τυράννων ,
sections.translation: And thou , poor wretch , who to thy sorrow art wedding a king ’ s daughter , little thinkest of the doom thou art bringing on thy children ’ s life , or of the cruel death that waits thy bride .

sections.label: Section 2.995
sections.original: δύστανε μοίρας ὅσον παροίχῃ .
sections.translation: Woe is thee ! how art thou fallen from thy high estate !

sections.label: Section 2.996
sections.original: μεταστένομαι δὲ σὸν ἄλγος , ὦ τάλαινα παίδων
sections.translation: Next do I bewail thy sorrows , O mother hapless in thy children , thou who wilt slay thy babes because thou hast a rival , the babes

sections.label: Section 2.1000
sections.original: σοι προλιπὼν ἀνόμως
sections.translation: thy husband hath deserted impiously to join him to another bride .

sections.label: Section 1002
sections.original: δέσποιν ’, ἀφεῖνται παῖδες οἵδε σοὶ φυγῆς ,
sections.translation: Thy children , lady , are from exile freed , and gladly did the royal bride accept thy gifts in her own hands , and so thy children made their peace with her .

sections.label: Section 1005
sections.original: ἔα .
sections.translation: Ah !

sections.label: Section 1005a
sections.original: τί συγχυθεῖσ ’ ἕστηκας ἡνίκ ’ εὐτυχεῖς ;
sections.translation: Why art so disquieted in thy prosperous hour ? Why turnest thou thy cheek away , and hast no welcome for my glad news ?

sections.label: Section 1008
sections.original: αἰαῖ .
sections.translation: Ah me !

sections.label: Section 1008a
sections.original: τάδ ’ οὐ ξυνῳδὰ τοῖσιν ἐξηγγελμένοις .
sections.translation: These groans but ill accord with the news I bring .

sections.label: Section 1009
sections.original: αἰαῖ μάλ ’ αὖθις .
sections.translation: Ah me ! once more I say .

sections.label: Section 1009b
sections.original: μῶν τιν ’ ἀγγέλλων τύχην
sections.translation: Have I unwittingly announced some evil tidings ?

sections.label: Section 1010
sections.original: οὐκ οἶδα , δόξης δ ’ ἐσφάλην εὐαγγέλου ;
sections.translation: Have I erred in thinking my news was good ?

sections.label: Section 1011
sections.original: ἤγγειλας οἷ ’ ἤγγειλας · οὐ σὲ μέμφομαι .·
sections.translation: Thy news is as it is ; I blame thee not .

sections.label: Section 1012
sections.original: τί δαὶ κατηφεῖς ὄμμα καὶ δακρυρροεῖς ;
sections.translation: Then why this downcast eye , these floods of tears ?

sections.label: Section 1013
sections.original: πολλή μ ’ ἀνάγκη , πρέσβυ · ταῦτα γὰρ θεοὶ
sections.translation: Old friend , needs must I weep ; for the gods and I with fell intent devised these schemes .

sections.label: Section 1015
sections.original: θάρσει · κάτει τοι καὶ σὺ πρὸς τέκνων ἔτι .
sections.translation: Be of good cheer ; thou too of a surety shalt by thy sons yet be brought home again .

sections.label: Section 1016
sections.original: ἄλλους κατάξω πρόσθεν ἡ τάλαιν ’ ἐγώ .
sections.translation: Ere that shall I bring others to their home , ah ! woe is me !

sections.label: Section 1017
sections.original: οὔτοι μόνη σὺ σῶν ἀπεζύγης τέκνων ·
sections.translation: Thou art not the only mother from thy children reft . Bear patiently thy troubles as a mortal must .
